title: OpenAI Gives the Government Access to Its Cyber Weapon. Anthropic's Already Leaked.
subtitle: GPT-5.4-Cyber is being briefed to US agencies and Five Eyes partners under a 'dual-track' access model. Meanwhile, Anthropic's rival Mythos model was accessed by unauthorized users through a vendor the same week it launched.

sources.angle: Exclusive: OpenAI briefed roughly 50 federal cyber practitioners in DC this week, running demos of GPT-5.4-Cyber. Uses a dual-track access model: broader public version with strong guardrails, more permissive version for vetted defenders.

Technical breakdown of GPT-5.4-Cyber deployment: OpenAI's Trusted Access for Cyber program vets governments and commercial users through the same process. Sasha Baker, OpenAI national security head, described plans to share threat intelligence across sectors.

Exclusive: Unauthorized users accessed Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview through a third-party vendor environment the same week Anthropic announced the model. Mythos can autonomously discover zero-days and chain multi-step exploits.

Analysis: The Mythos breach exposes how offensive-capable AI escapes through vendor chains, not core infrastructure. The breach raises questions about whether even highly restricted access programs are structurally secure.

brief.whatHappened: OpenAI held a classified demo in Washington this week for approximately 50 federal cyber practitioners from national security agencies, briefing them on GPT-5.4-Cyber under its Trusted Access for Cyber program. The model, released last week under a tiered access scheme, is being offered in two variants: a more constrained public version and a more permissive version for vetted defenders. OpenAI is briefing Five Eyes allies through the same vetting process. In parallel, Anthropic's rival Mythos model, restricted to a consortium of 40 elite companies due to its offensive capabilities, was accessed by unauthorized users through a third-party vendor environment on the day Anthropic first disclosed the program publicly.
brief.bottomLine: Two labs are racing to embed offensive-grade AI into government security infrastructure before anyone has agreed on who should control it, under what rules, or what happens when it leaks. One of them already leaked.

hiddenBets.assumption: The 'dual-track' model keeps offensive capabilities contained
hiddenBets.whyItMightBeWrong: Anthropic's Mythos breach shows that the attack surface is vendor chains, not the core model. Every additional vetted partner is a new attack vector. OpenAI's 'Trusted Access' program expands that surface deliberately.

hiddenBets.assumption: Governments are the right bodies to receive these capabilities first
hiddenBets.whyItMightBeWrong: Local water utilities and regional infrastructure operators are the most vulnerable targets, but also the least able to properly secure advanced AI tools. Giving them access first, as OpenAI's Chris Lehane suggested, trades coverage for control.

hiddenBets.assumption: Defensive use cases and offensive capabilities can be cleanly separated in a single model
hiddenBets.whyItMightBeWrong: A model that can autonomously chain zero-days for defense can do the same for offense. The distinction lives in the instructions given to the model, not in the model itself. Any actor who gains access gains both.

brief.realDisagreement: OpenAI chose broad access with safeguards. Anthropic chose narrow access without safeguards being sufficient. The fork is real: maximum coverage of defenders means maximum exposure if the chain breaks. Narrow access means faster attackers beat defenders to the tool. OpenAI's bet is that the safeguards hold; Anthropic's experience this week suggests they don't. On balance, Anthropic's restricted approach is right in principle but failed in execution, which suggests the actual choice is between two versions of inadequate control.
brief.whatNoOneIsSaying: Both companies are positioning government access as a public safety measure. But briefing the NSA and Five Eyes on your most powerful offensive tool is also the fastest path to avoiding future regulation. If the government depends on your product for national security, it will not regulate you out of the market.

whoPays.how: They will be last in line for vetted access but first in line as attack targets for adversaries who obtained the same model through a vendor breach.

whoPays.who: Third-party vendors and contractors in the cybersecurity supply chain
whoPays.how: They become the most exploitable link. The breach of Anthropic's Mythos came through a vendor environment, not the lab itself. Every expansion of vetted access multiplies the vendor surface.
Once OpenAI and Anthropic are embedded in federal cyber infrastructure, procurement naturally flows to those relationships. The government contracting advantage compounds.

scenarios.name: Race to embed
scenarios.whatHappens: Both OpenAI and Anthropic sign multi-year federal cyber contracts. Congress does not pass AI security legislation before the contracts are in place. The government becomes structurally dependent on frontier lab access before any oversight framework exists.
scenarios.signal: First formal federal procurement contract with either lab, expected by Q3 2026.

scenarios.name: Breach forces restriction
scenarios.whatHappens: A second Mythos-style incident, or a GPT-5.4-Cyber model exfiltration through a vetted partner, triggers a public congressional hearing. Access programs are frozen pending an executive review.
scenarios.signal: Any credible claim of unauthorized use by a non-vetted actor who obtained access through a third party.

scenarios.name: Allies diverge
scenarios.whatHappens: European Five Eyes partners, particularly the UK and Canada, impose domestic restrictions on using US frontier AI in sovereign cyber programs after the Mythos incident. The Five Eyes cyber-AI sharing arrangement fractures.
scenarios.signal: Any formal statement from GCHQ, CSE, or ASD declining to participate in OpenAI's Trusted Access program.

brief.whatWouldChange: If OpenAI or Anthropic published the full technical specification of their access controls, the independent audits, and the breach response protocols in detail, it would be possible to assess whether the safeguards are real or theatrical. The current opacity makes that judgment impossible.

markets.question: Will Anthropic have the best AI model at the end of April 2026?
Will Anthropic or OpenAI IPO first?
